answers: Tests always run faster, with no downside
The machine saturates, often making tests slower and flakier
The framework refuses to start
Sharding is automatically enabled

title: Final Automation QA project
short_description: A capstone project where you build a complete, CI-integrated automation framework for a real-style web app and deliver it like a job-ready engineer.
learning_goals:
Design and implement a maintainable test framework (Page Object Model, fixtures, config) for both UI and API layers.
Integrate the suite into CI (GitHub Actions) with reporting, artifacts, environment variables, and merge gates.
Apply scaling practices: Dockerized runs, parallel/sharded execution, and environment-driven configuration.
Deliver a documented, reviewable project (README, test plan, traceability) that demonstrates job-ready quality.
theory:
This capstone ties together every topic in the module into one deliverable: a working automation framework for a real-style web application (for example an e-commerce demo like saucedemo.com or a to-do/CRUD app), wired into CI, reporting, and run at scale. The goal is not a pile of test scripts but an engineered project a hiring manager or team lead would accept — structured, documented, reliable, and reproducible.

Scope. Pick an app with login, a list/catalog, and a multi-step flow (e.g. add to cart and checkout, or create/edit/delete a record). Cover it at two layers: UI tests for the critical user journeys (login, the happy-path flow, and at least one negative case like invalid login), and API tests for the backend endpoints behind those flows (auth returning HTTP 200 with a token, creating a resource returning 201, fetching a missing resource returning 404). Aim for roughly 15-25 meaningful tests — enough to demonstrate structure and parallelism, not so many that they are padding.

Architecture. Apply the patterns from the course: a Page Object Model so locators and page actions live in page classes, reusable fixtures/setup for authentication and test data, and a central config (baseURL, timeouts, retries, reporters, projects) overridden by environment variables. Tests must be independent so they run in parallel: unique data per test, fresh sessions, no order dependence. Secrets (any API token, test credentials) come from environment variables and a gitignored .env / CI secret store, never from code.

CI and scaling. The suite runs on GitHub Actions on every push and pull_request: checkout, setup with caching, install browsers, run the tests across a matrix or with sharding for speed, and upload an Allure/HTML report plus screenshots/traces as artifacts using if: always(). At least the core tests are a required status check that gates merging. Optionally containerize the run with the official Playwright image so local and CI environments match. Reports should include on-failure screenshots and on-retry traces so any red run is diagnosable without re-running.

Deliverables and acceptance. The project is judged like real work: a README explaining how to install, configure (env vars), run locally, run in Docker, and read the report; a short test plan / traceability note mapping each requirement to its test(s); green CI on a pull request with the report artifact attached; correct use of POM, config, parallelism, and secrets; and at least one demonstrated negative test and one intentional-failure run showing the report, screenshot, and trace working. The bar is: another engineer can clone the repo, set the env vars, and get the same passing run with an inspectable report — that is what 'job-ready' means.
key_terms: capstone framework, Page Object Model, fixtures, UI + API coverage, CI integration, Allure/HTML reporting, parallel/sharded execution, environment configuration, traceability, merge gate
real_world_example: You join a team and are asked to 'set up automation for the checkout flow from scratch.' That request is exactly this capstone: choose the critical journeys, build a POM-based framework with fixtures and a config, cover UI and the API behind it, wire it into GitHub Actions with reports and artifacts, make it run in parallel and in Docker, gate the merge on it, and hand over a README so the rest of the team can run and extend it. Delivering this capstone is rehearsing your first real automation task.
step_by_step:
1. Choose the target app and write a one-page test plan listing the user journeys and API endpoints to cover.
2. Scaffold the framework: install the runner, set up the config (baseURL, timeouts, retries, reporters, projects) with env-var overrides.
3. Build Page Objects for each page and reusable fixtures for auth and test data.
4. Write UI tests for the critical journeys plus at least one negative case; write API tests asserting correct status codes (200/201/404) and payloads.
5. Make every test independent: unique data, fresh sessions, no order dependence, so the suite runs in parallel.
6. Add reporting: Allure or HTML plus JUnit XML, with on-failure screenshots and on-retry traces.
7. Create the GitHub Actions workflow: triggers, caching, browser install, matrix/sharding, run, and if: always() artifact upload; reference secrets via ${{ secrets.* }}.
8. Optionally containerize with the Playwright image and run the CI job inside it.
9. Make the core tests a required status check on PRs.
10. Write the README and traceability note, open a pull request, and verify a green run with an attached report artifact.
common_mistakes:
Writing many shallow, copy-pasted tests instead of a smaller, well-structured POM-based suite with real coverage.
Hardcoding URLs, credentials, and waits in tests instead of using config and environment variables.
Submitting tests that only pass serially and break under the parallel CI run.
Forgetting if: always() so reports/artifacts are missing on the exact failing runs that matter.
No README or traceability, so a reviewer cannot install, configure, run, or understand what is covered.
practical_use_case: This is the deliverable you produce when joining a project that has no automation yet, or when a portfolio/interview asks you to demonstrate end-to-end automation skills: a complete, CI-integrated, documented framework that the team can run and extend from day one.
summary: The capstone integrates the whole module: a POM-based framework covering UI and API for a real-style app, configured via environment variables, reported with Allure/HTML plus screenshots and traces, run in parallel/sharded inside CI with merge gates, and delivered with a README and traceability. Acceptance means another engineer can clone it, set env vars, and reproduce a green, inspectable run.
example:
```
Acceptance checklist (capstone)
[ ] App + scope: login, one multi-step flow, one negative case chosen
[ ] 15-25 meaningful tests across UI and API (assert 200/201/404 where relevant)
[ ] Page Object Model + reusable fixtures for auth/data
[ ] Central config: baseURL, timeouts, retries (CI?2:0), reporters, projects
[ ] Env vars for BASE_URL + secrets; .env gitignored, .env.example committed
[ ] All tests independent -> pass under parallel workers
[ ] Reporting: Allure/HTML + JUnit, on-failure screenshots, on-retry traces
[ ] GitHub Actions: push+PR triggers, caching, browser install, matrix/shard
[ ] Artifacts uploaded with if: always(); report visible on the PR
[ ] Core tests = required status check (merge gate)
[ ] (Bonus) Dockerized run using the official Playwright image
[ ] README (install/config/run/Docker/report) + traceability table
```

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml (capstone CI core)
name: e2e
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix: { shard: [1, 2] }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: '20', cache: 'npm' }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx playwright install --with-deps
      - run: npx playwright test --shard=${{ matrix.shard }}/2
        env:
          BASE_URL: ${{ secrets.BASE_URL }}
          API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.API_TOKEN }}
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with: { name: report-${{ matrix.shard }}, path: playwright-report/ }
```
interactive: Draft a one-page project plan for your capstone: name the target app, list the 3-4 user journeys and 2-3 API endpoints you will cover, the test count target, the patterns you will use (POM, fixtures, config), and the CI/reporting/scaling features you will include. Keep it to a single page.
expected_answer: A focused plan that names a concrete app (e.g. saucedemo.com), lists critical journeys (valid login, add-to-cart + checkout, and a negative case like locked-out user) and the API endpoints behind them with expected status codes (login 200 + token, create 201, missing resource 404), targets ~15-25 tests, and commits to specific architecture (Page Object Model, auth/data fixtures, central config with env overrides) and operational features (GitHub Actions on push/PR, Allure/HTML + JUnit reporting, on-failure screenshots and on-retry traces, parallel workers and/or sharding, secrets via the secret store, merge gate). The plan should be realistic in scope and map each journey to planned tests, showing the learner understands the full pipeline rather than just writing scripts.
homework: Build and submit the complete capstone: the framework repository (POM, fixtures, config), UI + API tests (15-25, including a negative case and correct status-code assertions), environment-variable configuration with a gitignored .env and committed .env.example, a GitHub Actions workflow with reporting/artifacts/merge gate and parallel or sharded execution, optional Dockerization, and a README plus a traceability table. Open a pull request showing a green CI run with the report artifact attached, and include one demonstration of an intentional failure that produces a screenshot and trace in the report.
expected_result: A reviewable repository where another engineer can clone it, set the documented env vars, and reproduce a green run. It must show: a clean POM structure and fixtures; central config with env overrides and CI-only retries; independent tests that pass in parallel; UI and API coverage with real assertions (including 404 and at least one negative UI case); a working GitHub Actions pipeline with caching, matrix/sharding, if: always() artifact upload, secrets used safely, and a required-check merge gate; an Allure/HTML report with on-failure screenshots and on-retry traces; and a clear README and traceability table mapping requirements to tests. The intentional-failure demo should prove the report, screenshot, and trace machinery works end to end.
